model_input:
Question: A 65-year-old man presented with worsening right eye pain and purulent discharge. He reported being hit in the face by a tree branch several days prior. His medical history was notable for HIV, with a last known CD4 lymphocyte count of 112 cells/mm3 and variable adherence to antiretroviral therapy. At an outside hospital, cultures were obtained, and treatment was initiated with ofloxacin eye drops, 0.3%, ciprofloxacin hydrochloride ointment, 0.3%, and oral moxifloxacin hydrochloride, 400 mg daily. His symptoms worsened, and he was referred 3 days later for further evaluation.On examination, visual acuity in the right eye was hand motions. The left eye examination results were unremarkable. Slitlamp examination was limited by patient discomfort but revealed purulent discharge, a markedly inflamed conjunctiva, diffuse cornea opacification and thinning, and a flat anterior chamber. Given the history of tree branch trauma, the patient was brought to the operating room. Intraoperatively, a 0.5-mm corneal perforation was identified within an area of thinning (Figure, A). Microbial cultures were repeated, and corneal glue was applied. Gram stain of the purulent discharge demonstrated numerous polymorphonuclear leukocytes only. All subsequent microbiology cultures were negative. Despite treatment with topical fortified vancomycin, 25 mg/mL, tobramycin, 13.3 mg/mL, intravenous levofloxacin, 750 mg daily, and doxycycline, 100 mg twice daily, the purulent conjunctivitis and corneal stromal necrosis did not improve (Figure, B).Right eye. A, Marked purulence (asterisk) is noted under the sterile drape along the upper eyelid and stretches between the arms of the eyelid speculum. Marked corneal inflammation is evident. The central portion of the arrowheads point to a 1.8 2.2-mm area of marked corneal thinning within which is a smaller area of perforation. B, This optical coherence tomographic (OCT) image shows marked corneal thinning and areas (asterisk) of corneal stromal necrosis.Obtain initial culture results from the referring physicianReturn to the operating room for therapeutic keratoplasty What Would You Do Next?
Options: A:Obtain initial culture results from the referring physician B:Pursue serologic workup for autoimmune disease C:Begin empirical treatment for acanthamoeba D:Return to the operating room for therapeutic keratoplasty
 Which option is the correct answer to the question? ALWAYS provide the option index of the final answer AT THE END with TRIPLE BACKTICKS among A, B, C, D, EXACTLY like this: The answer is ```X```. DO NOT add any other text or variations.
opa: Obtain initial culture results from the referring physician
opb: Pursue serologic workup for autoimmune disease
opd: Return to the operating room for therapeutic keratoplasty
answer: Obtain initial culture results from the referring physician
explanation: Given persistent marked purulence and stromal necrosis, the negative culture results were surprising. However, these microbiology studies were obtained while the patient was receiving broad-spectrum topical antibiotic therapy. Initial culture results were obtained from the referring physician (choice A), which grew Neisseria gonorrhoeae. The patient then disclosed that his partner recently had a penile ulcer. He was immediately treated with saline lavages and single doses of intramuscular ceftriaxone sodium, 1 g, and oral azithromycin, 1 g, for gonococcal keratoconjunctivitis.Although this patient was initially transferred with concern for a traumatic ruptured globe, the history of prior tree trauma was a red herring. The persistent purulence and corneal stromal necrosis raised concern for infection. Yield of ocular surface cultures are decreased when samples are obtained with concurrent topical antimicrobial therapy.1 This case also emphasizes the potential importance of follow-up on primary data. When patients are transferred between institutions with different medical record systems, information can be lost. Serologic workup for autoimmune keratitis (choice B) is not the preferred answer because the hyperpurulence and beefy conjunctival injection would be atypical for this sterile inflammatory presentation. Starting empirical treatment for acanthamoeba (choice C) is incorrect because hyperpurulence is unusual in acanthamoeba keratitis, and the patient had no freshwater risk factors or history of contact lens wear. With progressive corneal necrosis, returning to the operating room for therapeutic keratoplasty (choice D) may have been required; however, the initial best step in management is to obtain confirmative diagnosis.Gonorrheal keratoconjunctivitis is due to infection with N gonorrhoeae, a gram-negative diplococcus. In populations other than neonates, transmission mostly occurs via direct sexual contact with infective secretions or indirectly via fomite transmission.2 A study in Ireland found the prevalence of gonococcal conjunctivitis to be 0.19 cases per 1000 patients evaluated for eye emergencies, with the majority in young adult males.3Treatment of gonorrheal keratoconjunctivitis is both systemic and local. Recommended systemic treatment includes administration of intramuscular ceftriaxone, 1 g, in a single dose.4,5 Systemic azithromycin, 1 g, in a single dose or oral doxycycline, 100 mg twice a day for 7 days, is also added owing to frequent coinfection with Chlamydia trachomatis.6 Topical treatment is ancillary and can include a fluoroquinolone, macrolide, and/or saline lavages.3,7 Gonorrhea is a reportable disease. Notification of risk of this disease and treatment of all sexual partners is necessary.Gonococcal keratoconjunctivitis may lead to serious ocular complications. Corneal stromal necrosis can occur, as with this case, in a fulminant fashion. Treatment of corneal stromal necrosis depends on the size and extent of stromal involvement and whether perforation of the cornea is present. Medical management of corneal stromal necrosis includes systemic antigonorrheal antibiotic therapy and anticollagenase medications. Surgical management for descemetoceles and perforations may include glue, amniotic membrane transplant, therapeutic lamellar, or full-thickness corneal and conjunctival flaps. In this case, corneal gluing was pursued because the area of perforation was small.The diagnosis of gonococcal keratoconjunctivitis should be considered in individuals who present with markedly purulent conjunctivitis with or without genital symptoms. History may not be forthcoming. Presentation classically includes conjunctival injection, eyelid edema, hyperpurulent discharge, and preauricular lymphadenopathy. With prompt, effective treatment, the infection can be eradicated and complications, such as corneal melt and perforation, can be avoided.After treatment with intramuscular ceftriaxone, 1 g, oral azithromycin, 1 g, gentle saline lavage, and topical moxifloxacin, 0.5% four times a day, this patient s anterior chamber reformed, the corneal epithelial defect decreased in size, and iris structures were visible owing to improvement in the corneal infiltrate. The patient was subsequently discharged from the hospital and lost to follow-up thereafter.
